planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full to partial sun; pearl bush establishes readily but benefits from consistent moisture during its first season to develop a strong root system before summer heat.

directions: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to partial sun; pearl bush thrives in most average garden soils and tolerates clay provided drainage is adequate, but performs best with moderate supplemental irrigation during dry spells in its first two years. Exochorda racemosa is a multi-stemmed, arching Chinese ornamental shrub celebrated for its spectacular spring bloom: in April and May the entire plant becomes blanketed in pure white, five-petaled flowers borne in long racemes, rivaling any flowering shrub in the garden. Prune immediately after flowering if needed to maintain shape, as flower buds are set on the previous season's wood; removing up to one-third of the oldest stems to the ground each year keeps the plant vigorous and open.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full to partial sun; oriental photinia establishes readily and is quite drought-tolerant once rooted, but consistent moisture in the first season promotes faster establishment and stronger flowering the following spring.

directions: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full to partial sun; oriental photinia is adaptable to a range of soil types and is notably more disease-resistant than the commonly planted red-tipped photinia. Photinia villosa is a deciduous shrub or small tree from East Asia offering outstanding multi-season interest: flat clusters of small white flowers appear in spring, followed by persistent bright red berries that last well into winter, and the foliage turns a brilliant red-orange in autumn that rivals the best fall color of any shrub in the temperate garden. Birds, particularly thrushes and waxwings, relish the persistent berries through late winter, making this an excellent choice for a wildlife-supporting mixed shrub border.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seed in fall into a prepared site in well-drained to moderately moist soil in full to partial sun; American vetch readily self-sows once established and can also be sown in early spring, though fall sowing allows cold stratification to improve germination rates the following spring.

directions: Direct sow scarified seed in fall into well-drained to moderately moist soil in a sunny to partially shaded meadow or woodland edge; nick or lightly sand the seed coat before sowing to improve germination, and inoculate seed with an appropriate rhizobium inoculant to activate nitrogen fixation from the first season. Vicia americana is a perennial scrambling vine native to meadows, prairies, roadsides, and open woodlands across most of North America, producing clusters of 4–9 purple-blue pea-like flowers from April through July that attract native bees and bumblebees while fixing atmospheric nitrogen into the soil through root nodules. It is an important larval host for the Gray Hairstreak butterfly and several other Lepidoptera, and its lightweight tendrils allow it to weave through grasses and low shrubs without smothering neighbors, making it an excellent addition to naturalistic meadow plantings.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring in moist to wet, acidic soil along streambanks, pond edges, or other consistently moist sites in full to partial sun; gray alder establishes vigorously in wet conditions and should not be planted in dry or well-drained garden soil where it will struggle.

directions: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring in moist to wet, acidic soil along streambanks, pond edges, or other consistently moist sites in full to partial sun; gray alder establishes vigorously in wet conditions and should be sited where roots will have reliable access to subsurface moisture or periodic flooding. Alnus incana is a fast-growing native tree of northern North America, prized for its ability to fix atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis with Frankia bacteria and to stabilize eroding streambanks with its dense, fibrous root system; male catkins emerge in early spring before the leaves, providing an important pollen source for early-flying native bees. The persistent small woody cones that remain through winter supply seeds to goldfinches, siskins, and redpolls, and the bark and foliage support a rich diversity of Lepidoptera larvae including many specialist moth species of northern forests.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seed in fall into sandy, rocky, or gravelly well-drained soil in full sun; Nuttall's milkvetch is adapted to naturally disturbed, nutrient-poor ground and requires excellent drainage — it will rot in heavy, wet, or clay soils.

directions: Direct sow scarified seed in fall into sandy, rocky, or gravelly well-drained soil in full sun; nick the hard seed coat lightly before sowing to improve germination and inoculate with a legume rhizobium inoculant to encourage nitrogen fixation from the outset. Astragalus nuttallianus is a native annual of the south-central United States — primarily Texas, Oklahoma, and New Mexico — producing small purple pea-like flowers in spring on low, spreading stems that thrive in the same disturbed, calcareous, sandy soils where it naturally occurs along roadsides, cedar glades, and open rangeland. This inconspicuous but ecologically significant species is a documented larval host for the Reakirt's Blue butterfly, a specialist of leguminous plants found across the arid Southwest, making it a valuable addition to native wildflower mixes in its natural range.
